data.title: Nebius: The Constant Gamechanger
data.description:
We have been bullish on most of the dips that we have seen in Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS). We last called a December 2025 selloff a gift that would keep on giving when shares dipped under $100. Strong returns of over 35% have been had in that time, even as the market is still off of highs. Yes, another buying opportunity did emerge just weeks ago as stocks were hit following the global oil shock due to the Iran War. Make no mistake at all, by every valuation metric the stock is expensive, but the future growth is why there is such a premium. But folks, this company is the constant game changer. Just when we think we know the story, waves of new information land, changing the outlook. The rapid hyperscaling and massive deals that are being made position Nebius, which has about 20% short interest, to see more upside on these catalysts. This comes even as there has been dilution. That is a risk, but the capital is being used to fund expansion efforts. In this column we check back in but move to a hold rating. This stock swings, and on the next swing lower, we think you can scoop shares for another trade, taking advantage of the beta like members at BAD BEAT Investing.

We have covered this before when we talked about Nebius being the new AI champion, so we will not go into detail, but if this is your first time reading a column of ours about Nebius, we will briefly hit the high points. Folks, it's an Amsterdam-based AI-native cloud infrastructure provider. We talked about this before, but it emerged in July 2024 following the $5.4 billion split of Russian search giant Yandex's international assets. The company focuses entirely on providing high-performance GPU clusters, cloud infrastructure, and services for AI development, with a specific focus on training and inference for generative AI. Some like to call it a neocloud company, offering specialized infrastructure that makes it a bit different than with general-purpose, legacy cloud providers.

The long-term appeal here is that Nebius is building a full-stack AI platform, controlling everything from the physical data centers and server design to the software orchestration layer. It offers GPU-as-a-Service (or GPUaaS), in that the company leases high-end NVIDIA (NVDA) GPUs (H100, H200, and upcoming Blackwell platforms) to startups, researchers, and enterprises. It also has an AI factory model approach in that it builds specialized data centers designed specifically for AI compute density, sustainability, and efficiency. Further, it does in-house engineering, unlike so many competitors that buy off-the-shelf servers. Instead, Nebius designs its own racks and server infrastructure in collaboration with NVIDIA to optimize for cooling and performance. It also has its AI studio, which is like a SaaS environment for users to deploy open-source foundational models via API for inference and decision-making.

Since we started covering the company less than two years ago, Nebius has been aggressively scaling its footprint, aiming for multiple gigawatt-scale capacity by 2030. It has a number of global installations. Of course there is the one in Finland, which is its flagship data center, which utilizes server-generated heat to meet local heating needs. It has a GPU cluster at an Equinix data center in France. The expansion into the United States has been a key bullish theme. We know that in Kansas City, Missouri, it is working on a 1.2 GW AI campus, which is expected to be its largest U.S. facility. But it also has a 300 MW facility in New Jersey. It also is moving into the UK and Iceland as well. This is just the start of the expansion, in our opinion. Recently the company has had several advancements that position the company for further success.

The month of March was huge for Nebius. While a war was raging and stocks were hit, Nebius enjoyed more wins. One of these wins was again with Nvidia (NVDA), who is a major partner. NVIDIA announced that it will invest $2 billion into the company. Further, the two companies have formed a strategic partnership to develop and deploy the next generation of hyperscale cloud for the AI market. This is going to help it deploy 5 gigawatts of Nvidia systems by the end of 2030. The deal will expand AI factory design and support and help create an inference and agentic AI stack for developers and enterprises. This deal strengthened an important relationship to ramp up the deployment of Nvidia infrastructure across Nebius’s platform.

Within the same one-week period of this important deal with NVIDIA, a deal worth possibly 10x this amount was announced with Meta (META). A huge deal, really. It was announced that Meta had committed to spending up to $27 billion over the next five years to access advanced AI infrastructure from Nebius. So in the first part of this deal, Nebius is going to offer up $12 billion of dedicated capacity across multiple locations, based on one of the first large-scale deployments of the aforementioned Nvidia's Vera Rubin platform. This will begin in 2027, and if all goes well, Meta will purchase available compute capacity across future Nebius clusters up to a total of $15 billion over five years. One might think that is a lot of capacity, which it is, but other parties were going to be buyers, and remaining capacity after those sales will be available to Meta. This deal significantly expands upon the $3 billion deal the two companies made in late 2025.

While not as much fanfare as the two deals above, Nebius also partnered with CrowdStrike (CRWD), our favorite cybersecurity stock and long-time investment at BAD BEAT Investing. This partnership will see CrowdStrike’s Falcon cybersecurity platform being offered up on Nebius AI Cloud, helping to deliver higher-end enterprise-level security to the rapidly expanding AI environments.

Now, earlier in the column we talked about dilution risks. That is, more shares being issued, etc., in the future as a possible risk to shareholder equity. We did just learn recently that the convertible debt offering (which is dilutive when converted) was upsized not once but twice, and the company closed on the offering, raising a whopping $4.3 billion. This money will, of course, be used to continue the AI buildout campaign and, naturally, to construct data centers. This round had 1.250% convertible notes due 2031 and 2.625% convertible notes due 2033, and the initial buyer maxed out its option to buy, raising $2.58 billion on the 2031 notes. The 2033 notes brought in $1.75 billion. When combined with the NVIDIA deal and revenue from Meta, there is a huge pile of cash to fund the global expansion we have been talking about with this company.

For long-term investors, it is a wait and see on how the company executes. Clearly the news flow is very positive of late. This has been more of a trading stock the last year with notable peaks and valleys in the charts. That has been easy to take advantage of for double-digit gains. We think right now, at nearly $130, with all of this good news being priced in, shares are a hold, but on another very likely dip in this volatile market, we like buying shares. Yes, the stock is very expensive. Yes, there is competition. Yes, there is dilution. This has perhaps capped the stock from really breaking out. And we are still probably two years away from consistent profitability. So, investors may be waiting to see purchases bear fruit at today’s price of about $130, but the company is moving heavily in the right direction.

data.title: Global Market Perspectives: Hope For The Best, Ready For The Worst
data.description:
Key themes for 2Q 2026

  • Crosscurrents and risks confront the global economy

The latest Middle East conflict and energy shock are posing downside risks to global activity and upside risks to global inflation. As a net energy exporter, the U.S. is the least vulnerable. By contrast, Europe and Asia are very exposed.

  • The U.S. economy is likely to cool slightly as consumers bear the brunt of higher oil prices

Although the U.S. economy still displays solid macro foundations, tightening financial conditions are likely to create challenges across the economy. Increased gasoline prices almost fully offset the benefits of the OBBBA tax refund.

  • The Federal Reserve is still set to cut rates, while other central banks lean toward hikes

The labor market has softened, but inflationary concerns imply the Fed will delay rate cuts until late 2026. The ECB and the Bank of England target price stability, so they have less flexibility to tolerate upside inflation risks and will not cut rates this year.

  • Equity market resilience is being tested

The path forward for U.S. equities is challenged, but the economy’s underlying resilience and the strength of corporate margins suggest that any drawdown should remain contained. While the recent outperformance of non-U.S. equities has stalled, once the conflict ends, international markets will offer compelling opportunities at attractive valuations.

  • Fixed income: Tight spreads, but benefiting from robust macro tailwinds

Pricing out of central bank cuts is driving front-end sovereign yields higher, while longer-end yields should trend lower as economic activity slows. Solid balance sheets and stable leverage should limit credit spread widening.

  • Focus on resilience and diversification

Another shock to test global growth resilience

Last year, economic strength across key regions—the U.S., Europe, and China—defied expectations that tariffs, lingering inflation, and geopolitics would suppress growth. As 2026 unfolds, the global economy is once again navigating several macro cross-currents that are testing its resilience.

While the macro foundation remains solid, cracks are emerging. Concerns around trade policy, private credit, and AI-related disruption continue to curb sentiment. More recently, the Middle East conflict and resulting spike in energy prices have introduced a new layer of uncertainty and fear.

The U.S.’s status as a net energy exporter implies greater resilience to energy shocks. As a result, U.S. growth downgrades have been minimal. In comparison, as a net energy importer, the European growth outlook has deteriorated more significantly. China, while heavily exposed to the Middle East, benefits from sizable oil reserves, the ability to shift toward coal, and declining energy intensity, helping limit the impact on its growth outlook.

Yet, if energy prices remain very elevated, it would push up global inflation, dampening global activity. More severe growth downgrades for key global regions—including the U.S.—would be expected.

After navigating multiple cross-currents in the first quarter, the global economy now faces a renewed growth risk from higher energy prices.

U.S. macro strength at risk of disruption

The U.S. economy entered 2026 on a solid footing, following a firm 2.1% GDP print in 2025, which was broadly in line with the long-term 2000s average. Improving business sentiment and robust consumer spending have bolstered the outlook, and the acceleration in AI-related investment has provided the economy with an additional growth engine.

Despite broad resilience, the key segment yet to gain traction is employment, likely contributing to very depressed consumer sentiment. Policy uncertainty, affordability strains, and concerns about AI disruption are also weighing on confidence.

The path ahead for the economy now likely hinges on the duration of the Middle East conflict and how long energy prices remain elevated. A sustained shock can seep into economic fundamentals, potentially altering the Fed’s policy trajectory and heightening the risk of a downturn.

Indeed, the recent sharp tightening in financial conditions reflects waning expectations for Fed easing. Financial conditions are still meaningfully more accommodative than during last year’s “Liberation Day” shock, but further tightening from here is likely and will create challenges across the economy.

The economy’s ability to withstand numerous headwinds since last year underscores underlying resilience supported by a strong capex cycle and robust consumer spending.

U.S. policy: the dessert may not taste as sweet as hoped

Last year’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) is set to deliver powerful fiscal tailwinds. The benefits should boost the average household’s tax refunds this year by more than $750, providing a solid short-term boost to consumption.

However, the current oil price shock could offset these benefits, as higher energy prices effectively function as a tax increase on households. At $80 p/b, the additional energy expense would amount to roughly $400 per household over the year. At $90 p/b, the gains from the OBBBA would be fully eroded for the average household.

The distributional effects are even less favorable. While the OBBBA’s benefits are skewed toward higher-income households, lower-income households are disproportionately exposed to rising energy costs. As a result, it would only take oil prices to settle at around $75 per barrel to offset the OBBBA’s benefits for the bottom 40% of households.

For businesses, the OBBBA offers capex incentives in the form of generous tax deductions, reducing the effective corporate tax rate from 21% to as low as 15%. If this is reinvested into capex, the benefit could extend throughout 2026. Yet rising energy and commodity costs could limit the tailwinds for overall corporate investment.

The OBBBA tax benefits are likely to be offset by rising oil prices, reinforcing K-shaped economic dynamics and limiting the effectiveness of corporate tax incentives.

Strong U.S. foundations are crucial shock absorbers

The U.S. economy’s strong underlying fundamentals suggest it can absorb a modest energy shock. Household balance sheets have strengthened materially, providing a meaningful buffer against macro headwinds. Since the pandemic, household net worth has risen by roughly $70 trillion.

That said, these gains have been unevenly distributed. Wealth accumulation has been concentrated among higher-income households, which have benefited disproportionately from strong capital markets. The top 20% of households now hold around 80% of total U.S. net worth, while lower-income households have a far more limited cushion and continue to face rising affordability pressures. Even so, aggregate consumption should remain resilient, as the top 10% account for nearly half of total consumer spending.

A key vulnerability is that today’s household strength is heavily equity-driven, leaving balance sheets more exposed to market volatility. However, sharp market drawdowns are uncommon when corporate profitability is healthy, and today’s corporate fundamentals remain solid: leverage is contained, liquidity is ample, and margins are near cycle highs.

Tightening financial conditions will pressure profits, but the corporate sector has crucial buffers against downside risk.

Household and corporate balance sheet strength provides ample buffers to the energy shock. The K-shaped economy is a concern, but it does not yet imply aggregate weakness.

Secular forces shape low-hiring, low-firing backdrop

The pace of job creation has continued to slow, heightening concerns about a slowing economy. Annual job gains slowed from 1.4 million in 2024 to just 182,000 in 2025. Yet, with the unemployment rate climbing only slightly and layoffs still very low, there is more than just weak cyclical demand at play. Indeed, two major secular forces are shaping the low-hiring, low-firing environment and keeping the labor market in balance:

  1. AI: There is no evidence of broad-based job displacement. However, entry-level workers, whose tasks are easier to automate, are having a harder time finding work, underscoring the notion that companies can maintain, or increase, output without expanding headcount. Certainly, the sharp divergence between job openings and earnings growth suggests that productivity gains may be at play.
  2. Demographics and immigration: Retiring baby boomers and lower immigration flows are reducing the number of available workers. Thus, the economy does not need to generate as many jobs to keep unemployment stable.

Given the risks to growth, labor demand still needs to be closely monitored, yet widespread layoffs are unlikely if earnings growth remains positive and profitability persists.

Job creation has slowed, but structural supply constraints continue to keep the labor market in equilibrium, even as macro headwinds weigh on labor demand.

Oil price shock: back to the 1970s inflation surge?

Inflation remains a challenge. Although it has trended lower, inflation has held above the Federal Reserve’s 2% target for five years and is now confronting a new shock.

The tariff-driven increase in inflation is widely expected to peak by mid-year. However, the current energy price spike complicates the path towards further disinflation and implies a second inflation wave may be approaching.

Federal Reserve models estimate that each $10/bbl increase in oil prices raises headline inflation by 0.3ppt. A sharp but brief spike in energy prices should only have a minimal impact.

However, a medium oil shock scenario, where oil stabilizes at $90/bbl, could see inflation rise to over 3.5% by year-end. Furthermore, a more severe oil shock scenario, which assumes a multi-quarter closure of the Strait of Hormuz, driving oil sustainably above $125/bbl, would push inflation to over 6% by year-end. There is also potential for large second-round effects, given the Strait’s downstream impact on food and semiconductor supply chains.

With history offering a clear warning, Federal Reserve is likely to proceed with heightened caution. The oil price shocks and early easing in the 1970s created dual surges in inflation that took several years and an eventual recession to tame.

Inflation pressures from tariffs should peak by mid-year, yet the inflation outlook is contending with another shock: the conflict in Iran, and its impact on oil prices.

Federal Reserve: Delayed, not derailed

Since the oil shocks of the 1970s and 1980s, the Fed has generally avoided tightening rates in response to supply-driven inflation, aided by inflation targeting and improved central bank credibility. However, the Fed was widely criticized for responding too late to the inflation surge following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, and, with inflation having remained above target for five years, its sensitivity to persistent energy shocks is likely to have heightened.

The Fed’s dual mandate implies strong consideration of both inflation risks and demand destruction. Continued labor-market softening and affordability pressures argue for an eventual return to a neutral fed funds rate.

Yet, with inflation still elevated and now facing upside risks from both tariffs and energy prices, the Fed is likely to proceed cautiously and will require clear evidence that core inflation has moderated and inflation expectations remain anchored before it cuts. As Chair Powell recently noted, without visible progress on inflation, cuts are off the table.

We expect the Fed to delay its next cut to September—if not December—with a second 25bps cut pushed into early 2027. This is a more dovish outlook than market expectations, which were even expecting a rate hike this year.

Inflation concerns imply the Fed will be more cautious about proceeding with its rate-cutting cycle. We expect the Fed to delay its next cut to September, if not December.

Global economy: Tailwinds despite energy vulnerabilities

Before the recent geopolitical flare-up created uncertainty for energy-importing countries, the global macro outlook was fairly constructive. In Europe, economic momentum had been picking up. PMIs are back in expansion territory, helped by easier financial conditions and fiscal expansion. However, Europe’s reliance on imported energy has dampened the outlook, and surging energy prices are weighing on activity. Notably, however, Europe is less vulnerable than during the 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine, as the region has diversified its energy supply chains.

In China, stronger-than-expected exports and its growing role as a tech innovator have offset the domestic headwinds from its real estate downturn. Even risks tied to the recent energy price shock are relatively contained, given China’s substantial reserves, flexibility to turn to coal, and surge in renewables.

Broader Asia, however, is heavily exposed to the energy shock due to its reliance on energy imports through the Strait of Hormuz. The immediate risk is the physical availability of commodities. Taiwan is the most vulnerable, while Japan and Korea have more cushion. Asia’s strength in tech innovation should provide an important buffer for growth, while fiscal stimulus is also likely in several Asian economies.

China’s export strength and tech innovation offset its real estate headwinds. In Europe, momentum was picking up but energy vulnerabilities pose a risk to the constructive outlook.

Global central banks: a more hawkish, asymmetric path

The energy shock has led markets to price out additional rate cuts this year, in some cases swinging from expected rate cuts to rate hikes.

Our views are slightly more dovish. We still expect the Fed to lower policy rates this year, albeit slightly later than originally projected. The ECB and Bank of England (BoE) solely target price stability, so they have less flexibility to tolerate upside inflation risks. We expect the ECB to raise policy rates once, perhaps twice, in 2026 (compared to market expectations of two hikes), and for the Bank of England to keep policy rates unchanged (compared to market expectations of two hikes).

However, forecast uncertainty is unusually high. While a short-term resolution is possible, a prolonged conflict with sustained energy price pressures would likely elicit firmer policy responses.

The oil price shock has renewed interest in the dollar’s safe-haven properties, prompting it to strengthen in recent weeks. Yet, divergent central bank policies and ongoing concerns about U.S. exceptionalism amid a new U.S. foreign policy playbook are likely to weigh on the greenback once the conflict ends—provided global growth has not been hit too hard.

Markets now expect almost all major global central banks to hike rates in 2026. This marks a meaningful shift in projections since the start of the year.

Equities

U.S. equities: vulnerable, but a relative global safe haven

Prior to the geopolitical escalation, market leadership had broadened beyond U.S. big tech, underscoring a risk-on backdrop favoring cyclicals. International equities ground higher, reflecting strength across Europe, China, and Japan, coupled with concerns over concentration risk in the S&P 500.

The tone shifted in March. The energy shock rattled equity indices across energy-importing regions, notably Europe and Asia. Meanwhile, the S&P 500 reemerged as an equity safe-haven, supported by the U.S.’s status as a net energy producer and its concentration in profitable, high-growth companies favored by investors when growth becomes scarce.

The path forward hinges on whether U.S. corporate margins can continue to absorb shocks, and whether further geopolitical flare-ups create broader economic spillovers and a potential shift in the Fed’s policy trajectory. Such conditions would likely prompt investor caution, as the landscape would no longer offer broad support across markets.

The uncertain backdrop underscores the importance of diversifying sectoral exposure amid various macro cross-currents. Industries poised to capture AI cost savings or with durable macro drivers, like pharmaceuticals or defense, may provide an important buffer should macro headwinds persist.

Renewed macro uncertainty underscores the importance of diversification, with stocks backed by secular drivers and resilient fundamentals better positioned to absorb shocks.

AI rally: Solid fundamentals; careful selection warranted

Before geopolitics took the center stage, the tech sector’s underlying vulnerabilities had been the key focus. Last fall, surging debt-funded AI-related capex raised fears of bubble-like dynamics, triggering a valuation pullback that slowed tech’s uninterrupted rally. Concerns deepened in February as rapid advances in AI models amplified fears that AI solutions could displace legacy business models, triggering a sharp selloff across tech and services-related industries.

The indiscriminate selling created more attractive entry points, while also easing bubble concerns, as valuations retraced and sit well below dot-com extremes.

The sector’s resilience has re-emerged amid rising geopolitical tensions—an indication that investors still see strong balance sheets and growth stocks as buffers against uncertainty. However, the sector is not immune to energy disruptions linked to Middle East tensions, and a prolonged disruption could strain semiconductor supply.

Looking ahead, careful selection in tech remains critical. AI-driven disruption is real, and as AI progresses, the dispersion between leaders and laggards across both tech and services is likely to widen. Firms with adaptable business models and clear pathways to productivity gains through AI adoption are positioned to resist, and potentially thrive, in the transition.

Investors see valuation opportunities following February’s tech correction, though heightened scrutiny implies more dispersion within the sector.

Seeking diversification in a complex macro backdrop

The recent run-up in non-U.S. equities has stalled amid the latest bout of volatility, reviving investor appetite for U.S. equities. However, investors must remain mindful of U.S. concentration risk, as maintaining diversification into non-U.S. equities, especially where valuations are more appealing, can help foster a more balanced portfolio.

Europe: Low-tech exposure offers diversification benefits, notably in Germany, given its heavier weight in industrials and financials. Yet, the region’s sensitivity to energy shocks, which has driven the recent valuation pullback, is a headwind. Elevated geopolitical tensions warrant selectivity, with more resilient opportunities tied to defense and energy security.

Asia: While dependence on Middle Eastern energy imports remains a key vulnerability for several Asian economies, China’s ample reserves and diversified electricity mix leave it better positioned to absorb higher energy costs. Beyond the conflict, cheaper valuations, domestic policy support, and AI advances underscore Asia’s opportunities.

LatAm: Cheaper valuations largely reflect the region’s history of policy instability, yet richness in critical minerals and metals provides long-term advantages as global demand for resource-intensive technologies rises.

U.S. equities remain the poster child of strong growth. But policy upheaval and tech concentration warrant broader exposure, particularly in regions with domestic catalysts.

Fixed income

Global upturn in yields is underway

Interest rates had trended lower at the start of the year, but the outbreak of hostilities in the Middle East has created a perfect storm for bond markets. Global yields have pushed higher since the end of February, with the U.S. 10-year yield approaching 4.50% at the end of Q1. Much of the action has been focused at the short end of the yield curve, however.

As energy prices have surged, inflationary concerns have risen, leading to a hawkish repricing of central bank expectations across most developed economies. In effect, this dynamic has pushed short-end yields higher.

While there has also been some hawkish pass-through to 10-year yields, their move has been more modest. Growth worries amid the energy shock are likely providing some offset. Indeed, a prolonged period of elevated energy prices would spell demand destruction and weigh on long-end yields, helping to flatten the yield curve.

U.S. yields have been relatively more insulated amid this market dynamic. U.S. inflation expectations remain anchored, while the Fed’s enduring credibility, despite numerous challenges this year, has also contained upward pressures. By contrast, rising long-term UK inflation expectations have sharply pushed up long-end gilt yields.

Bond yields have been moving steadily higher, though pressures have been more pronounced at the short end, leading to an overall flattening of the yield curve.

Credit resiliency in a challenging backdrop

Recent downside shocks have added to headwinds, triggering a risk-off environment that has widened credit spreads since the start of the year. Yet, provided the earnings environment remains robust, healthy margins should continue to underpin credit markets, limiting spread widening. Fundamental drivers such as solid balance sheets and stable leverage should also provide buffers against risks.

Concerns have risen about private credit, especially amid several high-profile lender bankruptcies and amid AI disruption that is spotlighting software exposure among lenders. However, these risks remain largely idiosyncratic, supported by the still-constructive macro foundations. Moreover, despite private credit’s linkages to the traditional banking system, spillovers to financial stability should be manageable, as banks today are much better capitalized than during prior crises. There is also little sign of stress passing through into other parts of the credit market.

Emerging market debt has outperformed since Inauguration Day, as a weaker dollar and Fed rate cuts have preserved relative-value opportunities in the space. However, the energy shock could limit EM central banks’ policy space to continue easing and disproportionately weigh on growth.

A robust earnings environment and healthy corporate fundamentals point to corporate credit weathering the multiple shocks that it is facing.

Investment perspectives

Resilience and disciplined diversification

Market sentiment had already softened early in 1Q amid AI-related uncertainty and private credit concerns, leaving risk assets vulnerable. Against this backdrop, the energy shock has triggered a synchronized global risk-off move.

Even so, the underlying strength of the global economy—supported by resilient household and corporate balance sheets and continued earnings momentum—suggests that any drawdown should remain contained. History also argues against abrupt portfolio shifts in response to geopolitical events. While our constructive medium-term outlook remains intact, the current environment reinforces the importance of resilience and diversification across asset classes and regions.

Portfolios should remain positioned for global growth, while maintaining exposure to assets that tend to perform well during periods of heightened risk aversion. These include high-quality assets, selective commodities, and sectors such as defense that typically benefit from elevated geopolitical tensions. As a net energy exporter, the U.S. economy is also relatively less exposed to higher oil and gas prices.

In an increasingly fragmented and volatile global landscape, disciplined diversification remains one of the most effective tools for navigating uncertainty while staying aligned with long-term objectives.

The underlying strength of the global economy suggests that any drawdown should remain contained.

Balancing resilience amid geopolitical and inflation crosscurrents

Renewed energy price volatility and supply shortages amid geopolitical escalation are testing market confidence, even as underlying economic and corporate fundamentals remain resilient. The environment reinforces the need for disciplined exposure, diversification, and an emphasis on quality across portfolios.

Equities

Favor resilience and diversification as leadership becomes more selective

  • Prioritize earnings durability over multiple expansion, favoring companies with strong balance sheets and cash flows.
  • Maintain AI exposure while broadening beyond hyperscalers, targeting beneficiaries of adoption, productivity, and capex.
  • Selectively add U.S. small- and mid-caps, where valuations remain more attractive and sensitivity to domestic growth is higher.
  • International equities provide diversification at cheaper valuations and should be supported by improving earnings growth momentum and a softer USD once the conflict ends.

Fixed income

Navigate a hawkish repricing with quality, income, and curve discipline

  • Maintain IG exposure, supported by resilient earnings, strong balance sheets, and limited spread widening risk.
  • Stay selective in high yield, where defaults remain contained, but dispersion argues for active management.
  • Private credit risks remain largely idiosyncratic, with limited spillover given strong bank capitalization and solid macro.
  • Emerging market debt offers relative value, though energy shocks may constrain policy easing in vulnerable economies.

Alternatives

Diversify concentrated portfolios with real assets and private markets

  • Private real estate remains selective, with strong fundamentals in data centers, healthcare, residential, and logistics.
  • Public REITs look increasingly compelling, benefitting from income stability and duration support as growth slows.
  • Multi-strategy and real return alternatives enhance resilience, helping hedge inflation and policy volatility.

Implementation

  • Resilient active managers
  • Active small-cap strategies
  • Active global and emerging market strategies
  • IG credit heavy core allocations
  • Flexible emerging market debt
  • Preferred and capital securities
  • Active high yield strategies

  • Private real estate markets
  • Proven REIT strategies
  • Multi-strategy alternatives

data.title: Intel Foundry Services Is Materializing As The Turnaround Of The Decade
data.description:
Intel Corporation (INTC) is positioned for a major turnaround, as Intel Foundry Services [IFS] gains momentum in sourcing third-party chip designers following the announced partnership with Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), to potentially manufacture its advanced AI chips that will power autonomous driving, its Optimus humanoid robot, and SpaceX (SPACE) satellite constellation. With optimism growing for the once financially strapped chip foundry, Intel’s positioning in the market may be returning to a strong footing in the coming years, potentially turning to profitability by eFY28. Given the optimistic outlook for Intel, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating with a price target of $93/share at 16.12x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

Intel Corporation Operational Update

Intel has been circulating in the headlines after a blockbuster announcement that the once financially strapped technology giant may be in a position for a major turnaround. In early April 2026, Intel announced a partnership with Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), to bring CEO Elon Musk’s vision to light and develop an advanced chip fabrication facility to design, fabricate, and package purpose-built logic and memory chips to power SpaceX and Tesla technologies. Musk first announced plans for the Terafab in early 2026, around the time of the Tesla Q4 ’25 earnings release, hinting that the automaker would bring in-house an advanced semiconductor fabrication facility to manufacture a large proportion of the chips needed to power these autonomous engines.

Fast forward to April 2026; Musk announced a partnership with Intel to leverage its chip fabrication expertise to potentially manage the entire process net of design. This can be a major paradigm shift for Intel, which has been digesting years of overinvestment in capital equipment while clinging to the hope of attracting external semiconductor designers for foundry services. From a thematic perspective, this can be Intel’s path into further fabrication of custom silicon under multi-year partnership agreements, providing a runway to tape out multiple nodes and multiple generations of custom silicon for the next generation of compute. From Tesla’s perspective, Intel can provide its expertise in advanced packaging to streamline production for purpose-built chips for its robotaxi program, Optimus humanoid robots, and radiation-protected chips for satellite systems.

A partnership with Intel may also help Tesla accelerate chip production, where the initial plan was to ramp up to 100,000 wafers per month before scaling to 1 mm/month at full capacity. In terms of the development cadence, Tesla will likely begin volume production of the AI5 chip in mid-2027 as it develops the next generation, AI6, which may be ready for production in late 2027 and deployment in 2028. Given the timing of chip development, this could potentially align with Intel’s fabrication business, which may require capital equipment sourcing and other preparations to run at mass production. Details relating to process technology have not been disclosed, but if timing aligns and is suitable for Tesla’s chips, the chips could be manufactured either on 18A or 14A. Intel is planning to be in risk production of Intel 14A in 2028 and ramp to volume production in 2029, potentially aligning with Tesla’s development cadence.

In addition to its partnership with Tesla, Intel is currently in talks with Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(GOOGL) to provide advanced chip packaging services, each of which could be major deals for Intel Foundry Services. Intel will be leveraging its latest iteration of Embedded Multi-die Interconnect Bridge-TSV [EMIB-T] technology for advanced AI and HPC chip nodes, which is expected to improve semiconductor designs’ performance, power efficiency, and flexibility, supporting HBM4 memory stacks, all developed using the Synopsys, Inc. (SNPS) 3DIC Compiler. Accordingly, Intel is preparing for scaled production, which may align with management’s comments of ramping up third-party chip production while speaking at the Second Annual AI Summit.

Deals with Amazon and Alphabet may be major revenue drivers, as each of the companies is set to deliver large quantities of training and inferencing chips to Anthropic over the coming years. Alphabet recently announced an agreement with Anthropic to deliver 3.5 GW of compute capacity built on Google TPUs. Amazon, on the other hand, recently announced committing 2 GW of Trainium compute capacity for OpenAI.

Broadening its capabilities, Intel recently announced a definitive agreement to repurchase the 49% equity interest in Fab 34 in Ireland from Apollo for $14.2b. Thematically, this can be an appealing acquisition given the growing importance of CPUs for AI inferencing as enterprises turn towards leveraging AI applications and agentic AI.

Intel Corporation Financial Forecast

As a result of the new optimism towards sourcing external customers for IFS, I am increasing my revenue and profitability forecast for the segment, expecting IFS’s profitability to turn positive in eFY28. Despite the optimistic outlook for IFS, I do not expect a significant uptick in growth until late eFY26-27 for its existing third-party customer, eFY27-28 for the hyperscalers, and eFY28 for Tesla and the hyperscalers.

For eQ1’26, I’m forecasting Intel to report $12.5b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $0.04/share, maintaining my previous forecast for the quarter. The big shift in sentiment is more geared towards its long-term growth potential for IFS, which may turn around from being a cash incinerator to a cash-generating machine.

Risks Related to Intel Corporation

Bull Case

Intel is gaining momentum in sourcing third-party IFS customers, a major transition from the challenges previously faced after years of heavy investments in capital equipment. With the shift in tone towards disciplined capital spending, Intel may also be turning towards positive free cash flow in the coming fiscal years; I’m forecasting the transition to occur in eFY27.

Bear Case

Intel is still in the early stages of talks with Amazon and Google, and agreements may not materialize. While Tesla may be a major partner to deliver substantial growth for IFS, Tesla has a history of pushing back timelines, which may impact Intel’s IFS business.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

INTC shares are trading at a 1-year high at 21.61x EV/aEBITDA, a relative premium when compared to the shares’ historical trading range. Despite the high trading premium, I believe the renewed optimism for external IFS services may deliver stronger growth over the coming years, potentially resulting in a significant mispricing of shares.

Fundamentally, I believe INTC shares should be priced at $93/share at 16.12x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA; I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating.

Using the model, the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: Oil Just Doesn't Want To Correct With Persistent Ceasefire Uncertainty - WTI Technical Analysis
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Traders were just celebrating the latest 2-week US–Iran ceasefire news, but it now seems that markets got ahead of themselves.

The initial reaction was one of a classic risk unwind – WTI crude oil gapped sharply lower from around $118 to $92 as risk premium evaporated almost instantly on the announcement. For a brief moment, it looked like the geopolitical overhang that had fueled oil’s explosive rally was finally unwinding.

But that relief didn’t last.

Since the gap lower, prices have been grinding steadily higher again, now hovering back near the key $100 level — a sign that traders are reassessing just how fragile this truce really is.

Headlines over the past 24 hours have painted a highly uncertain picture:

Iranian officials have stressed that any meaningful peace agreement must extend beyond bilateral talks, explicitly mentioning broader regional considerations, including Lebanon, and warning that the coming hours are “critical.”

At the same time, conflicting narratives between US and Iranian sources have only confirmed that the two sides are still far from trusting each other. What is sure is that traffic in the Strait of Hormuz remains more than muted, if inexistent.

A recent Reuters report indicated 6 crossings in the last 24 hours compared to the usual 140 in the same timespan – that's not the type of progress that markets want to see.

This kind of environment is particularly difficult for commodity pricing – oil thrives on clarity of supply expectations, but current conditions offer anything but that. Instead, participants are stuck in a feedback loop of headline-driven volatility, where each new comment shifts sentiment without establishing a durable trend.

The result is a market caught in limbo — no longer pricing worst-case escalation, but far from confident in a sustained de-escalation.

As WTI breaks the $100 threshold yet again, the next directional move will likely depend less on technicals and more on whether diplomacy can turn from words into verifiable action. Until then, expect volatility to remain elevated and conviction to stay thin.

As the situation looks to be more clear in the coming days, let's dive into a multi-timeframe analysis of WTI (US) Oil to determine levels of interest and put the odds in the trader's favor to capitalize on the issue.

US Oil Multi-Timeframe Analysis

WTI 4H Chart and Technical Levels

WTI has seesawed after yesterday's large drop to $92.70, having already rallied by 11% since.

After touching the oversold RSI border and the bottom of the Pivotal War support (~$93), bulls have raged back into the commodity as uncertainty still looms – A very important test is now coming closer with the 4H 50-period MA ($104.62)

  • Breaching back above would put the bullish panic thesis fully back into action
  • Rejecting it, however, would allow a more rangebound action ahead ($98 to $104 until markets get more clarity)
  • Breaking $98 again hints at a lower range ($92.70 to $98)

WTI Technical Levels:

Resistance Levels

  • 4H 50-period MA / 1H 200-MA (~$104.20)
  • $106 to $108 June 2022 Resistance
  • $110 psychological level
  • 2022 and War highs $117 to $120

Support Levels

  • $98 to $100 Major Pivot
  • War Support Pivotal $93.00 to $95
  • $87 to $90 mini-Support (4H 200-period MA)
  • 2025 Highs Key Support $78 to $80
  • Pre-War Support $63.80 to $64

1H Chart and action levels

Looking closer, WTI is once again stuck in uncertain back and forth action.

The 200-hour MA ($104.18) will be acting as a major barometer for traders.

On the immediate action, the rebound in WTI is stalling as the 1H RSI is back to overbought.

  • Traders will surely await for further news before moving on to the next trades
  • Keep an eye on a potential rangebound formation from $98 to $104 as markets await for further clarity.

Keep a close eye on the latest headlines!

data.title: Genmab: Multiple Binaries Could Alter The Thesis
data.description:
It has been over a year since my last Genmab (GMAB) article, where I discussed Genmab’s pipeline, strategic partnerships, and impressive growth thanks to strong performances by DARZALEX and EPKINLY. Despite ongoing legal disputes with Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), I believed that the company’s upcoming Q3 earnings report was going to be a catalyst that could potentially reverse the 20% decline in the share price. Since then, the company has averaged double-digit revenue growth, and the ticker is up over 20%. However, the market’s sentiment still appears to be fixated on the company’s DARZALEX revenue, and the royalties are expected to come to an end in 2029 in the U.S. and the early 2030s in Europe. This is a major concern because DARZALEX has been the company’s primary revenue source for years, and we don’t know if the company has enough in the portfolio to offset those losses, much less continue to grow deep into the second half of the next decade. Well, I read through a recent healthcare conference presentation transcript, and it looks as if EPKINLY, Rina-S, and petosemtamab are all ready for registrational readouts that could establish Genmab’s multi-billion-dollar oncology platform and help reset the bull thesis away from DARZALEX.

Currently, GMAB has dropped under my Buy Threshold of $28.25, but a strong bounce off an anchored VWAP has me considering a buy in the near term just in case the market realizes the potential of Genmab’s pending catalysts.

I intend to discuss Genmab’s upcoming catalysts and how a clean run of positive data could transform GMAB’s bull thesis. Then, I will discuss how the Street expects Genmab to perform in the next decade. In addition, I will point out some of my leading downside risks that could come from these binary events. Finally, I take a look at the GMAB Daily Chart to see if I can identify an opportunity to add to my position while it is trading below my Buy Threshold.

Stacking Up Catalysts

As we move deeper into 2026, Genmab's outlook is centered on a pile of late-stage clinical catalysts that could define the company for the next decade. It looks as if Genmab has up to six registrational readouts slated over three lead assets (EPKINLY, Rina-S, and petosemtamab). Not only is this a substantial volume of data in a condensed timeframe, but we also have to acknowledge that these readouts will likely determine if these candidates will get through the FDA in a tight cadence. So, not only could these data readouts determine the probability of FDA approval, but we could see Genmab launching new products and indications leading to multiple value inflections.

EPKINLY’s Phase III readout in relapsed/refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) was released back in January, expected in early 2026, with EPKINLY showing a statistically significant improvement in PFS along with higher response rates and durability. However, it did not demonstrate an overall survival (OS) benefit (HR ~0.96). Although the OS miss is material, it might not prevent EPKINLY from being approved. Still, it might force a narrow label, slower adoption, and maybe require confirmatory data for full approval.

The EPKINLY DLBCL data is expected to be followed by additional combination and earlier-line studies through the first half of the year. A separate Phase III study in first-line DLBCL is also expected to report this year, which would represent a larger commercial opportunity given the larger patient population. Rina-S is expected to generate registrational Phase II data in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer in the second half of this year. Petosemtamab is expected to post Phase III data in head and neck squamous cell carcinoma at some point in the second half of the year.

The commercial opportunity of these candidates is substantial. Management outlined that EPKINLY's peak sales have the potential to exceed $3B if they can get the label expanded into earlier lines and global penetration continues in B-cell malignancies. Rina-S is projected to bring in $2B+ in peak sales from gynecologic oncology, with potential upside as it expands into extra indications. Petosemtamab is seen as a multi-billion-dollar solid tumor asset, particularly if its early efficacy marks still hold up in larger studies. Indeed, some biotech companies can post rosy internal peak sales estimates, but I wouldn’t reject Genmab’s numbers because they have already demonstrated strong clinical and commercial prowess. In fact, Genmab’s proprietary product sales grew more than 50% year-over-year in 2025.

I will also point out that these data readouts and potential approvals over the next year are going to be critical to keeping investors enthusiastic about the long-term prospects of the company. Yes, Genmab is guiding to ~14% revenue growth in 2026, but we also have to consider that they are also expected to ramp up their OpEx to support late-stage development and prepare for the launch of these assets. So, positive data readouts can reassure the market that the pending compression from investment will be worth it for the possibility of multiple launches in 2027.

Changing the Thesis

Genmab came into 2026 with strong clinical and commercial momentum thanks to DARZALEX and EPKINLY, with 19% revenue growth and 24% operating profit expansion in 2025. Looking at the company’s earnings history below, we can see that Genmab has been able to roughly double their quarterly revenue in only a few years.

Sadly, the share price has done the opposite and has gone from trading around $40 per share in Q1 of 2022 to around $28 per share today.

Why?

Well, as I mentioned in my introduction, the DARZALEX revenue is expected to dry up between 2029 and 2032 because Johnson & Johnson has been winning the court battles. Before these court rulings, a GMAB investment looked asymmetrical with no clear ceiling for over a decade. Now, the market has an end date for the DARZALEX royalties, so the outlook got flipped… despite the company continuing to report strong growth.

However, 2026 and 2027 have the potential to change the outlook with multiple registrational readouts that could enable several high-value 2027 launches. Genmab’s thesis could change from a royalty- and milestone-driven thesis to wholly owned or partnered products where the company has a say in pricing, access, lifecycle management, and global rollout… unlike with DARZALEX. I know that might not seem like a big deal, but again, the ticker’s performance has been married to DARZALEX for years, so we are possibly looking at GMAB adding a layer of insulation from DARZALEX with every new approval and launch.

The question is… Can they offset the decline in DARZALEX royalty income?

Looking at the revenue estimates below, it appears as if some Street analysts are confident that Genmab will be able to offset the loss of DARZALEX income by 2029 and 2030.

Indeed, the estimated growth with DARZALEX is double-digit, and it goes down to single-digit growth without. However, the market has been treating GMAB as if the company is doomed, and there is limited upside on the horizon. I understand that the market has to be cautious about bidding a ticker up ahead of uncertainty, but the market has pushed the share price down to discount levels for its current and projected performance. GMAB is currently trading around 4x forward price-to-sales for 2026 and 2.5x forward price-to-sales for 2030. The industry average is typically around 4x-5x price-to-sales, so the ticker is discounted for any potential growth.

Of course, we have to assume the market is going to chastise the ticker because of the surge in OpEx and the consequential drop in EPS for this year. However, Street analysts are estimating an immediate rebound in 2027 and strong double-digit EPS growth for the remainder of the decade.

I understand these are just estimates, but they do illustrate how the company can absorb the costs of building a biotech company that can survive and possibly thrive after losing its primary breadwinner. Again, the company could have the registration data from the programs that are expected to fuel this growth this year, with the possibility of those assets being approved and on the market by the end of 2027. So, we are looking at a potentially rapid transition from a DARZALEX stock to a more diversified biotech company with an impressive list of technologies and partnerships.

Risks to Consider

Although I have confidence in Genmab’s ability to execute in the clinic and on the market, I must concede there is still a significant downside that could derail my transition thesis. First and foremost, I'm relying on multiple assets to produce registration-level data that will lead to approval. Having more than one asset heading towards the finish line is in itself very impressive, but the company needs these programs to be approved if they want the product to be on the market and gain commercial traction in time to offset the looming threat of losing their DARZALEX income. Again, I have confidence in Genmab’s ability to handle these assets in the clinic, but we have to accept that producing homerun data in oncology is incredibly difficult… and even then, the data might have to be undeniably superior to the current standard of care to get the best label or be a commercial success. So, Genmab needs the 2026 data readouts to be more than just “acceptable” if they want to offset DARZALEX and continue growth in the next decade. It is possible that only one failure could devastate the share price for a prolonged period of time, because even if the company is successful with the remaining programs, the loss of one might be enough for them to fall short.

I also have to acknowledge that the risk doesn’t disappear if the data is positive and should support approval. These programs will still have to deal with several commercial risks, such as competition, payers, adoption curves, etc. So, it is possible that GMAB’s potential resurgence could lag the progress until the evidence is indisputable that all of the programs are a commercial success, and they are tracking to fill the void left by DARZALEX. Essentially, I have to see these programs as a single, high-stakes binary event.

Despite these risks, I am maintaining my GMAB conviction rating at 4 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare “Bioreactor” portfolio.

My Plan

In my previous Genmab article, I disclosed that I would not be haphazardly clicking the buy button despite GMAB trading below my Buy Threshold of $28.25 and Buy Level 1 of $23.11. However, I was able to load up on shares back in late November of 2024 and again in May 2025, but I ceased my buy activities once the ticker went on a run in late 2025. Now, the ticker is back below my Buy Threshold and has shown a strong bounce off a long-term anchored VWAP around $25 per share and is holding above the anchored VWAP from the April 2025 low.

So, I do see some indications that GMAB might be ready to return above my Buy Threshold, and possibly go on a run if these data readouts are positive. Therefore, I’m going to be on the lookout to make a minor addition to my GMAB position in the coming days, just in case the market wants to take the share price out of my price range.

If I do make a small addition, I will then look to set equivalent sell orders around my Sell Target 1 of $42 per share so I can book profits and return my position to a "house money" condition.

On the other hand, if the company reports lackluster data, I will go hands-off on the ticker until the company can address the concerns and the market resets the trading range.

Long term, I still expect to trade GMAB for at least five more years to produce profit while developing a core position for a long-term investment.

data.title: Q4 GDP Revision And February PCE: Growth Revised Down, No Relief On Inflation
data.description:
The latest batch of data reinforces a familiar but increasingly uncomfortable macro setup: growth is softening at the margin, but inflation is showing little sign of easing. The final revision to Q4 2025 GDP confirmed that economic momentum slowed more than initially reported, largely due to temporary factors tied to the government shutdown. At the same time, more timely data on February income, spending, and prices point to a consumer that continues to drive activity despite weakening income growth, with firm demand coinciding with another step higher in inflation.

Q4 GDP

The third and final release of Q4 2025 GDP data provided an even more sour view of growth than the second estimate did. Real GDP is estimated to have grown at a SAAR of just 0.5% now, down from 0.7% in the previous release. The downward revision can be blamed on new, lower estimates of consumer spending and investment growth in Q4, while the final readings of contributions from government and trade were roughly the same. The broad story is the same from the previous release: the government shutdown caused the U.S. economy to grow at the second slowest pace since Q1 2022 (slowest was Q1 2025, caused by massive imports).

Let’s go over the two major downgrades in the data:

  • Personal consumption expenditures (PCE) grew at a SAAR pace of 1.9%, only down slightly from the previous estimate of 2.0%. This revision came from lower nondurable goods spending growth of 0.4% (down from 0.6%), offset by an upward revision in durable goods spending growth of 0.3% (up from 0.0%). The estimate of services spending growth was unchanged at 2.7%.
  • Gross private domestic investment growth was revised down sharply to 2.3% SAAR (from 3.3%), marking the largest downgrade among major components. The drag came from weaker inventory accumulation, with inventories contributing just 0.14 ppts (down from 0.28), while fixed investment contribution edged lower to 0.26 ppts (from 0.29), primarily reflecting softer residential investment.

Even though there were some changes to consumption and investment, the story of Q4 2025 is still the same. The government shutdown was the dominant driver of the slowdown, with government consumption contracting at a SAAR of -5.6% (revised up slightly from -5.8% actually) and the federal component alone subtracting -0.96 ppts from headline GDP growth. Outside of that drag, private sector activity held up relatively well, as real final sales to private domestic purchasers rose at a 1.8% SAAR pace (revised down -0.1 ppts), pointing to an economy that continued to hum along despite disruptions to incomes and spending tied to the shutdown.

This is also reflected in the alternate measure of growth, real gross domestic income (GDI), which rose at a 2.6% SAAR pace in Q4 2025, down from 3.5% in Q3. Averaging the two measures, real GDP and real GDI increased 1.5% in the fourth quarter, a notable slowdown from the 4.0% pace in the prior quarter, and further reinforcing that there was a step down in overall economic momentum but not a capitulation.

Personal Income and Outlays

The BEA also released more current data covering income, spending, and inflation in February. The headline personal income growth figure came in much lower than expected, falling -0.1% MoM when an increase of 0.3% MoM was forecasted. Data also showed that disposable personal income fell -0.1% MoM and, when adjusted for inflation, dropped -0.5% MoM. This is the largest decline in overall and disposable income since May 2025. The weakness in incomes came specifically from declines in personal income receipts on assets (-0.8% MoM) and personal current transfer receipts (-0.4% MoM), which were both only partially offset by employee compensation up 0.2% MoM.

Despite the drop in incomes, consumer spending remained firm in February. Personal consumption expenditures (PCE) rose 0.5% MoM, the strongest monthly gain since August 2025, driven primarily by a surge in goods spending. Goods consumption increased 0.9% MoM, led by durable goods (+2.0%) and a sharp rise in auto spending (+4.5%), while services spending continued to expand at a steady 0.3% pace. Under the surface, spending on core categories remained particularly strong, with PCE excluding food, energy, and housing up 0.6% MoM, suggesting that underlying consumer demand held up well even as income growth weakened.

A good portion of the nominal spending strength in February can be attributed to another firm step higher in prices, largely in line with expectations. The headline PCE price index rose 0.4% MoM, the largest increase since January 2024 and matching the pace seen in both January and February 2025, leaving the YoY rate unchanged at 2.8%. Core PCE also increased 0.4% MoM, matching January’s pace, while the annual rate edged down slightly from 3.1% to 3.0% YoY. The monthly strength was driven primarily by goods prices, which rose 0.7% MoM, including a 1.1% jump in durable goods. Nondurable categories were also firm, with clothing up 1.0% and gas and energy goods up 1.4%, while services inflation remained more contained at 0.2% MoM.

Taken together, these dynamics point to persistent inflationary pressure rather than a continued disinflation trend. Price gains are broadening across categories, supported by firm consumer demand and easier financial conditions following rate cuts in Q4 2025. There is little evidence of sustained cooling in the medium-term trajectory, especially as tariffs and input cost pressures continue to filter through. Importantly, these data are already somewhat outdated, as energy prices moved higher in March and supply chain pressures spiked due to the conflict in the Middle East, suggesting that inflation readings will remain elevated across a range of goods categories over the next few months.

Market Reaction

It is hard to tease out a reaction to specific macro releases when financial markets are so sensitive to geopolitical developments in the Middle East. Indeed, with the recent ceasefire being announced, investors are laser-focused on the disagreements between the U.S. and Iran and whether or not it will hold. The bond market is playing a similar game, but it has also been sending a clearer macro message over the last month: inflation is getting worse.

The month of March saw large rises in Treasury yields, with my charts focused on the 2-year (US2Y) and 10-year (US10Y) rates, up 41 bps and 37 bps, respectively. These gains were not only driven by oil prices skyrocketing and shipping lanes becoming more complicated by the Middle East conflict, but also by the Federal Reserve sending a hawkish message on rates in the March FOMC meeting. Indeed, the March FOMC minutes released yesterday suggested that rate cuts could be needed if inflation starts to spike.

“Many participants pointed to the risk of inflation remaining elevated for longer than expected amid a persistent increase in oil prices, which could call for rate increases to help bring inflation down to the Committee's 2 percent objective and keep longer-term inflation expectations firmly anchored.”

Today’s data points broadly support this upside risk. Yes, Q4 2025 GDP did get revised down, but there wasn’t too much to be taken from that update since the story remained the same (government shutdown impeded growth). Instead, the February income and outlays data drove the macro narrative today as it revealed that consumer remains willing to spend even if income growth is negative and prices consistently increase. All together, these macro forces support higher rates and a patient Fed over the next few months.
